title: Death rate from communicable vs. non-communicable diseases
subtitle: The estimated annual death rate per 100,000 people. Death rates are compared between [non-communicable diseases](#dod:non-communicable-diseases), versus infectious, maternal, neonatal and nutritional diseases combined.
note: To allow for comparisons between countries and over time, this metric is [age-standardized](#dod:age_standardized).
caption:
Death rate from communicable vs. non-communicable diseases

The estimated annual death rate per 100,000 people. Death rates are compared between non-communicable diseases, versus infectious, maternal, neonatal and nutritional diseases combined.

This scatter plot compares 2023 estimated annual death rates per 100,000 people for non-communicable diseases against infectious, maternal, neonatal, and nutritional diseases combined, using age-standardized metrics to allow for comparisons between countries and over time. Both axes are plotted on a logarithmic scale. The vertical axis ranges from 300 to over 1,000 deaths per 100,000 for non-communicable diseases, while the horizontal axis spans from 20 to 500 for communicable diseases. Countries are color-coded by region: North America in orange, South America in brown, Africa in purple, Europe in blue, Asia in teal, and Oceania in light blue. Circle sizes correspond to population, with a reference scale showing circles for 8 billion and 38 million. Nauru sits at the top of the chart with a non-communicable disease death rate exceeding 1,000, while Singapore appears near the bottom left with low rates on both axes. A cluster of lower-middle-income countries occupies the middle-right portion of the chart, including nations like DR Congo, Chad, and Nigeria, which show higher communicable disease death rates between 200 and 500. European countries such as Finland, Germany, France, and Italy cluster in the lower-left quadrant with non-communicable disease rates between 300 and 400 and communicable disease rates below 50. The world average is marked by a large gray circle positioned around 650 for non-communicable diseases and 150 for communicable diseases.
